title: Will AI fix prior authorization—or make it worse?
gist: AI is being deployed on both sides of the U.S. health-insurance prior-authorization fight: CMS is expanding AI-assisted review into original Medicare via the WISeR pilot even as it pressures private insurers to streamline prior auth, while critics warn the same technology risks scaling wrongful denials faster than it speeds legitimate approvals.

themes.theme: AI's Double-Edged Role in Prior Authorization

insights.claim: A 2025 American Medical Association survey found 61 percent of doctors worry that AI will exacerbate denials of treatments they consider medically necessary, even though AI could in theory speed approval of unambiguously allowable claims.
insights.speaker: Undark Magazine
insights.so_what: Widespread physician distrust of AI-driven claims review signals political and regulatory risk for insurers and AI vendors selling into this workflow.
insights.quote: 61 percent of doctors worrying that AI will exacerbate denials of what they deem are necessary treatments.
insights.locator: A 2025 American Medical Association survey of physicians

insights.claim: The AMA is advocating that insurers be required to provide detailed clinical reasoning to justify AI-influenced denials and to disclose more transparency about the algorithms used.
insights.speaker: Undark Magazine
insights.so_what: Mandated algorithmic transparency and denial-reasoning requirements would be a direct compliance cost for insurers and AI-in-healthcare vendors if adopted.

insights.claim: Health policy analyst Camm Epstein frames the core design risk as one of intent: AI in prior authorization should make appropriate care easier to approve, not make it easier to deny necessary care.
insights.speaker: Undark Magazine
insights.so_what: This is the litmus test investors and regulators should apply to any AI-in-claims-adjudication product: does it net-net approve more legitimate care or deny more of it.
insights.quote: AI should be used to make appropriate care easier to approve, not necessary care easier to deny.
insights.locator: email to Undark, health policy analyst Camm Epstein

themes.theme: WISeR: CMS's AI Pilot in Original Medicare

insights.claim: CMS's WISeR (Wasteful and Inappropriate Service Reduction) demonstration project runs through December 2031 across six states, pairing machine learning with human clinical review to scrutinize services CMS deems prone to overuse or fraud, including skin and tissue substitutes, electrical nerve stimulator implants, and knee arthroscopy for osteoarthritis — the first significant push of prior-authorization-style review into original Medicare, which had rarely used it before.
insights.speaker: Undark Magazine
insights.so_what: Original Medicare, previously largely free of prior-auth friction, is becoming a new addressable market and regulatory battleground for AI-driven utilization review vendors.
insights.locator: Centers for Medicare and Medicaid Services began a demonstration project called WISeR

insights.claim: Vendors participating in WISeR are paid a share of the 'averted expenditures' their AI-driven reviews generate, meaning they earn more revenue the more care requests they help reject — a structural incentive that has drawn lawmaker resolutions and amendments aimed at blocking the model's funding.
insights.speaker: Undark Magazine
insights.so_what: Pay-per-denial vendor economics is a red flag for anyone evaluating AI-in-utilization-review business models on conflict-of-interest grounds; legislative defunding risk is live.
insights.locator: vendors participating in the WISeR model

insights.claim: Investigations by the Washington Post, KFF Health News, and the Seattle Times, cited by researcher Zena Wolf, indicate that in its first few months WISeR has already caused care delays and denials in each of the six pilot states.
insights.speaker: Undark Magazine
insights.so_what: Early real-world evidence of harm within months of launch increases the odds of program modification, litigation, or defunding before the pilot's 2031 end date.
insights.locator: cited investigations by the Washington Post, KFF Health News, and the Seattle Times

insights.claim: Former Cigna executive turned insurance-reform advocate Wendell Potter tracked organized political pushback against WISeR before it even launched, indicating the model was contested from its inception rather than only after problems surfaced.
insights.speaker: Undark Magazine
insights.so_what: Pre-launch political opposition from a former industry insider suggests WISeR's legal and funding footing is unusually contested for a CMS demonstration project.
insights.locator: Before WISeR was implemented, Wendell Potter

insights.claim: Medicare Advantage now enrolls roughly 55 percent of Medicare-eligible seniors and disabled people, meaning prior-authorization friction in that program affects a majority, not a fringe, of the Medicare population.
insights.speaker: Undark Magazine
insights.so_what: Any prior-auth reform or AI adoption in Medicare Advantage now has majority-population-scale impact, raising the political stakes of getting it wrong.
insights.locator: insurers issue millions of full or partial claim denials annually

insights.claim: A 2022 HHS Office of Inspector General review found Medicare Advantage plans denied more than 1 in 10 requests that actually met coverage rules, yet 81 percent of denials were overturned on appeal in 2024 — implying most of the harm comes from the initial wrongful denial and delay, not from a permanent loss of coverage.
insights.speaker: Undark Magazine
insights.so_what: The appeal-overturn gap is the key metric to watch: if AI-driven initial denials rise while overturn rates stay high, the system is generating pure friction/delay rather than genuine cost control.
insights.quote: Medicare Advantage plans overturned 81 percent of denials upon appeal.
insights.locator: An HHS Office of Inspector General memorandum published in 2022

insights.claim: A Commonwealth Fund survey found roughly one in five working-age adults with private insurance reported that they or a family member were denied physician-recommended care in 2025; 41 percent of those denials delayed care, and more than a quarter said the patient's health problem worsened as a result.
insights.speaker: Undark Magazine
insights.so_what: These are the baseline harm metrics against which any AI-driven prior-auth reform (public or private) should be measured going forward.
insights.locator: A newly released Commonwealth Fund survey

insights.claim: A 2024 rule from the Biden administration requiring insurers to decide urgent prior-authorization requests within 72 hours and non-urgent requests within 7 calendar days took effect January 1, 2026 for most public-sector health plans, meaning binding federal speed limits on insurer decisions are only now becoming enforceable.
insights.speaker: Undark Magazine
insights.so_what: 2026 is the first year these enforceable timelines are live; investors and patient advocates should watch for compliance data and enforcement actions this year.
insights.locator: A rule issued by former President Joe Biden's administration in 2024

insights.claim: Private insurers pledged alongside the Trump administration to standardize electronic prior-authorization requests by 2027 and to reduce the volume of services subject to prior authorization by 2026, including common procedures like colonoscopies and cataract surgeries.
insights.speaker: Undark Magazine
insights.so_what: These are self-imposed, unaudited industry pledges rather than binding rules — worth tracking against actual 2026/2027 outcomes rather than taking at face value.
insights.locator: Private insurance companies vowed to standardize electronic requests by 2027

insights.claim: An industry survey reports prior-authorization request volume fell 11 percent between June 2025 and April 2026, but the same survey does not disclose whether the denial rate changed, leaving the key patient-harm metric unmeasured.
insights.speaker: Undark Magazine
insights.so_what: A drop in request volume without corresponding denial-rate disclosure could mask insurers simply pre-filtering requests rather than genuinely easing the process — a data gap worth pressing on.
insights.quote: requests for prior authorization declined by 11 percent.
insights.locator: The industry-based survey reveals that between June 2025 and April 2026

insights.claim: CMS Administrator Mehmet Oz has publicly warned insurance executives that they must ease prior-authorization burdens themselves or face imposed federal regulation, signaling escalating regulatory risk for the health-insurance sector independent of AI adoption specifics.
insights.speaker: Undark Magazine
insights.so_what: A credible threat of top-down regulation gives insurers a near-term deadline incentive to self-reform, which could compress margins tied to prior-auth-driven cost avoidance.
insights.quote: If you don’t do it yourselves, then we’re going to do it for you.
insights.locator: CMS Administrator Mehmet Oz has warned insurance company executives

insights.claim: In a 2025 industry-group survey, every responding health plan agreed with the statement that AI or algorithms without clinician review are not used to deny prior-authorization requests involving medical necessity — a self-reported, unverified assurance that sits uneasily alongside the financial incentives built into programs like WISeR.
insights.speaker: Undark Magazine
insights.so_what: Self-attested compliance statements from insurers are a weak substitute for independent audits; treat industry survey claims of 'no AI-only denials' with skepticism until externally verified.
insights.locator: Responding to an industry group survey conducted last year

insights.claim: Physician and Healthcare Huddle founder Jared Dashevsky argues that instead of reducing administrative waste, AI in prior authorization is fueling an 'arms race to deny faster and appeal faster,' automating and scaling a broken adversarial process rather than fixing it.
insights.speaker: Undark Magazine
insights.so_what: The bear case for 'AI fixes healthcare admin' products is that they simply speed up both sides of an adversarial loop rather than reducing total friction — a key due-diligence question for health-tech AI investments.
insights.quote: arms race to deny faster and appeal faster. More automation of a broken system that shouldn’t exist in its current form.
insights.locator: Jared Dashevsky, a physician and founder
